title: Key data are missing for millions of children
source: World Bank’s Learning Poverty and Country Learning Outcomes database (learning assessment); UNICEF/WHO/The World Bank: Joint child malnutrition estimates (JME) database (stunting); UNSD Population and Vital Statistics Report (birth registration)
note: Availability of data between 2019 and 2024 for the following indicators: births registered with a civil authority (% of children under five);
visdescription: Chart showing that key data are missing for children. Many children are living in economies lacking coverage in birth registries, learning assessments, nutrition data, etc.
keyFactLabel: children live in an economy with no recent internationally comparable learning assessment.

scenes.text: Around [emphasis: 195 million children] under five are unaccounted for in birth registries, making up about 30 percent of all children in this age group, globally.
These gaps are concentrated in specific regions. In [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], around half of all children under five were never registered at birth.

Around [emphasis: 207 million children] live in economies with no recent data on child growth, which are needed to measure malnutrition. In [emphasis: East Asia & Pacific] and in [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], stunting data—the primary measure of whether young children are adequately nourished—are missing for more than half of all children under five.

Around [emphasis: 778 million children] aged 5–14—roughly half of all children that age—live in economies with no comparable learning assessment in the past five years. In[emphasis: East Asia & Pacific], [emphasis: South Asia], and [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], there are no comparable data on whether more than half of these children can read or understand basic math at age-appropriate levels.

_meta.approvalNote: survey strip, Nigeria dual-line, waffles, SPI, SPI–GDP
